data.title: Franklin High Income Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: The fourth quarter (Q4) of 2025 saw monthly shifts in market sentiment as uncertainties increased in the first part of the quarter. The US federal government went into a shutdown on October 1 as the US Senate was unable to pass a spending bill. This, among other things, delayed or canceled the publication of key economic data leading to a lack of clarity on the economic condition of the United States. The shutdown was lifted in mid-November, and subsequent data showed that economic growth in the United States remained resilient. The US Federal Reserve delivered three consecutive 25-basis-point (bp) cuts in Q4, helping market sentiment to improve in December. Fixed income volatility measures fell toward the end of the quarter, reaching their lowest level since 2021. The US Treasury (UST) yield curve steepened during the quarter as the benchmark 10-year UST yield rose two bps to end the year at 4.17%.
  • Contributors: Security selection in healthcare and packaging.
  • Detractors: Security selection in chemicals and media cable.
  • Outlook: Overall, we remain constructive on high-yield (HY) corporate credit and believe it offers a reasonable loss-adjusted carry but acknowledge that valuations do not offer much upside potential from here.

Performance Review

  • The fund’s security selection contributed to returns led by selection in the healthcare, packaging and finance sectors. In contrast, selection within the chemicals, media cable and wired communication segments of the HY market curbed results.
  • Our industry exposures also helped performance. There were positive contributions from our underweight to technology and retailer sectors as well as an overweight to finance-related issues. Overweight exposure to building and underweight food and beverage names and overweight to the chemical sector curbed quarterly results.

Outlook

  • The high-yield market performed well in 2025. As we head into 2026, corporate fundamentals have been resilient and technicals in the market have been supportive. We expect these trends to persist, with bouts of volatility surrounding the implementation of President Trump’s policies.
  • From a valuation standpoint, spreads remain rich historically, but are appropriate given the low default environment supported by wide open capital markets. The Fed implemented 75 bps of cuts in the second half of the year as the labor market softened and growth slowed but remained positive.
  • We remain focused on industry and company fundamentals, the health of balance sheets, generation and use of free cash flow, the resiliency of credits to slower economic growth, and are cautious on deeply distressed names. The U.S. high-yield default rate (including distressed exchanges) increased in December, but remains well below long-term averages, ending the month at 1.88%.
  • Technicals remain solid with strong demand, inflows and consistent new issues. Yields and sub-par dollar prices remain somewhat attractive, while spreads remain at levels that are fair but not compelling.
  • Risks to our outlook include higher than expected inflation, policy missteps, a more severe slowdown or recession, and/or heightened geopolitical tension.

As of 12/31/2025 the fund’s Advisor Class shares received a 5 star and Class A shares received a 4 star overall Morningstar rating™, measuring risk-adjusted returns against 584, 548 and 445 High Yield Bond funds over the 3-, 5- and 10- year periods, respectively. A fund’s overall rating is derived from a weighted average of the performance figures associated with its 3-, 5- and 10-year (if applicable) rating metrics.

data.title: What Is HIMS Worth Without GLP-1? Earnings Preview
data.description:
Hims & Hers Health, Inc. (HIMS) is down around 50% year-to-date. And no, that is not a glitch. The stock is now down from every time I covered it, starting with the 50% plunge since I first started covering it back in late March of last year. While most of the aggressive sell-off started this year after a series of unfortunate events that I’ll get to in a second, the stock has been losing momentum since mid-October of last year, when it traded at $62 and never again revisited these levels or even came close. The stock was down around 50% from that level by early January this year, and as you already know, it continued selling off due to legal uncertainties.

The stock is finding a sweet spot at $16, and earnings are coming up, and while I don’t think it could get much worse for HIMS from current levels, I’m downgrading the stock to a hold. Sentiment on HIMS has shifted from aggressive growth to a wait-and-see approach due to legal and regulatory risks, and I recommend investors watch this one from the sidelines until regulatory clarity.

Let’s take a look at the timeline for a second.

A trip down memory lane:

The company reported Q3 2025 back in November, and the stock sold off despite HIMS reporting revenue up 49% year-over-year to $599 million as investors priced in the EPS miss at $0.06 against consensus at $0.09 and gross margins that came in at 74%, down from 79% from a year-ago quarter. The drop that followed was fueled by fears that the FDA would remove GLP-1 drugs from the shortage list, which would block HIMS from selling its cheaper compounded versions. These fears came to life earlier this year, and that’s when HIMS really dropped out of the market’s favor. Fast forward to this month, and things got worse. Here’s the GLP-1 war timeline:

February 21, 2025: The FDA officially removes Semaglutide from the drug shortage list, theoretically ending the legal loophole that allowed HIMS to mass-produce compounded versions.

April–May 2025: HIMS tries to pivot by striking a deal to sell branded Wegovy directly. However, they continue to sell their cheaper compounded version by claiming it is personalized (a different dosage/formulation), which is a gray area of the law.

June 23, 2025: Novo Nordisk (NVO) terminates its partnership with HIMS after only one month, accusing HIMS of "illegal mass compounding" and deceptive marketing. HIMS stock dropped 30% in a single day at the time and never recovered these losses.

January 5, 2026: Novo Nordisk launches the official Wegovy Pill (oral semaglutide) at a price of $149/month.

This is where the administration and the courts stepped in aggressively:

February 5, 2026: HIMS announces its own Compounded GLP-1 Pill for just $49/month, a direct shot at Novo's new $149 pill.

February 5, 2026 (Later that day): FDA Commissioner Marty Makary takes to X to say that the “FDA will take swift action against companies mass-marketing illegal copycat drugs, claiming they are similar to FDA-approved products.” The stock plunged another 10% after the post, and the fact that Novo Nordisk issued a public statement vowing to sue Hims, calling the pill an "inauthentic and untested knockoff."

February 6, 2026: The FDA officially announces its intent to restrict the active ingredients (APIs) used for these compounded drugs.

“Today, the U.S. Food and Drug Administration is announcing its intent to take decisive steps to restrict GLP-1 active pharmaceutical ingredients (APIs) intended for use in non-FDA-approved compounded drugs that are being mass-marketed by companies—including Hims & Hers and other compounding pharmacies—as similar alternatives to FDA-approved drugs,” said the press release. The Department of Health and Human Services referred HIMS to the Department of Justice for potential violations of the Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act.

February 7, 2026: Under federal pressure, HIMS cancels the sale of its $49 pill. Below is their post on X.

February 9, 2026: Novo Nordisk files its lawsuit in U.S. District Court, with the charge being patent infringement (specifically US Patent 8,129,343) and the evidence being its lab tests that found impurities of up to 86% in HIMS’ injectable products and 75% in their pills.

Past coverage:

I last covered the stock in late September and gave it a buy. Still, I told investors the following:

FDA scrutiny of compounded GLP-1 marketing and Trump's new tariff regime seem to be giving investors a little bit of uncertainty about what had been the company's fastest-growing revenue stream in GLP-1s. Even if the direct financial hit is minimal today, the stock has seen some volatility ahead regarding this. For investors, I think Hims is a good opportunity to hold a digital health disruptor, but it's not for the faint-hearted; investing in this stock means being comfortable with volatility in the near term and being willing to wait out regulatory overhangs.

I came to understand that I had underestimated the extent of the volatility that would follow the regulatory overhang. Looking back, the core mistake in my original HIMS coverage was not in identifying demand for GLP-1s or the company’s ability to scale distribution, but in underestimating how quickly the regulatory environment could flip to hostile. The compounding loophole was never a durable moat but a temporary regulatory gray zone. Once that gray zone closed, the entire profit pool underpinning the bull case evaporated almost overnight. The FDA’s actions, DOJ involvement, and Novo Nordisk’s litigation fundamentally altered HIMS’ risk profile in a way that no amount of marketing efficiency or subscriber growth could offset in the near term. Under this new regime, the market is repricing the stock and asking: What is HIMS worth without GLP-1?

While a big part of my investment thesis was around the weight-loss drug aspect of the business, the core business is showing healthy growth, though not hyper. Without GLP-1, revenue growth reverts closer to the company’s core trajectory. I think the base business can still compound at a healthy double-digit rate, driven by subscriber growth, category expansion (testosterone therapy, dermatology, mental health), and improved retention, but it no longer justifies growth multiples tied to obesity-drug TAM narratives.

Valuation and what’s next

After the aggressive sell-off, it goes without saying that HIMS is attractive at current levels from a valuation lens. On a sales basis, the stock now trades at 1.6x TTM revenue and 1.6x forward sales, well below the sector median. While EV/Sales and Price/Sales look attractive, EV/EBITDA and EV/EBIT remain stretched, which is understandable given the business's transition back to its core telehealth economics and the shift away from one of its highest-margin product lines.

HIMS continues to post sector-leading revenue growth, with 78% year-over-year growth and 46% forward growth, far outpacing the sector median. With everything said, the forward growth profile is now less clean. Long-term EPS growth expectations have deteriorated materially, reflecting uncertainty around how quickly management can replace GLP-1 economics with equally high-quality revenue streams. Stripping GLP-1 out of the narrative, HIMS should be valued as a scaled consumer health subscription platform rather than a structurally advantaged obesity-drug disruptor. At $16, the stock is no longer pricing in GLP-1 upside, and a large portion of the multiple reset has already occurred, which limits near-term downside barring further legal escalation. But upside from current levels is dependent on execution and regulatory clarity. So, while HIMS is cheap, it is cheap for a reason.

Until the company demonstrates that it can rebuild a premium profit pool without GLP-1, valuation upside will remain capped, and the stock is better framed as a watchlist name, not a high-conviction investment. HIMS reports Q4 2025 on February 23rd. The company guided Q4 revenue to $605 million to $625 million, with consensus at $619.19 million, up 28.7% year-over-year, and adjusted EBITDA to $55 million to $65 million. Consensus for EPS is at $0.02 per share, down 81.8% year-over-year.

For full-year 2025, management expects revenue of $2.34 billion to $2.36 billion and adjusted EBITDA of $307 million to $317 million. Longer term, on its last earnings call, management reaffirmed confidence in its growth trajectory and reiterated its 2030 targets of at least $6.5 billion in revenue and $1.3 billion in adjusted EBITDA, stating: “With the immense opportunity in front of us, our conviction remains high in our ability to meet or exceed the 2030 targets we established earlier this year.” With the regulatory unrest now up in the air, I'll be watching whether management remains confident in said guidance. Still, I’m telling investors to stay on the sidelines as the risk-reward at current levels is unfavorable.

data.title: Planet Labs: The Easy Money Is Gone
data.description:
Space is becoming the buzzword for 2026, and there’s good reason to get on board. Last year, global investments in space tech reached a record, surpassing the previous peak set in 2021. About 60% of that spending comes from the U.S., driven by hyper-investment in launch services and defense-related programs, and the Trump administration shows no signs of slowing down. Recently, President Trump signed an executive order “designating space as a core national security and economic priority.” The revived obsession with space is due to its being thrown into the ring with AI and defense: the holy trinity for 2026. Planet Labs (PL) stands out at this intersection.

The company, founded in 2010 by three ex-NASA scientists, focuses on earth observation and, to date, operates the largest fleet of Earth-imaging satellites, providing a gold mine of data. PL went public in late 2021 and hovered in the sub-$10 range until its recent 2025 breakout, as shown in the chart below. The company’s edge and subsequent breakout moment came from its transformation into an AI-driven data company, not only collecting data but also processing data on-orbit using AI. PL has been working with Google (GOOG)(GOOGL) since going public and has partnered with Nvidia (NVDA) since 2024 to integrate Jetson AI modules into its Pelican and Owl satellites. Excitement around PL’s opportunity has been building quickly, with the stock now up 240% over the past year and about 216% over the past six months.

Part of the surge in excitement around PL is due to increased attention to space itself. Simply put, PL isn’t feeling the love alone. Space stocks have been on the rise since early January, driven by the buildup to SpaceX's anticipated IPO, expected as early as June, and reinforced by the company's acquisition of xAI, which brought the combined valuation of the two to $1.25T. Over the past two weeks, however, it seems space is falling out of favor with investors, and the hype is shifting elsewhere following the valuation run-up. PL has pulled back approximately 25% from its high of $30.90 per share, currently hovering around $22 per share. The growing concern is the gap between the stock's current price and its fair value, estimated at $11 per share. The question on everyone's mind is, what to do with PL now?

The chart below shows the surge and pullback in some retail-favorite space stocks over the past six months.

PL is acting like a momentum stock, moving with the broader market tug-of-war between greed and fear. Within the space-related peer group shown above, we believe PL has a differentiated long-term opportunity, driven not just by the sheer volume of earth-imaging data it holds but by the intelligence it has turned this data into. The company hosts an extensive archive of Earth intelligence enabled by owning the largest Earth-imaging satellite fleet worldwide. PL’s core product is a daily scan of the Earth, or basically high-frequency global monitoring. The benefit of this strong daily earth-monitoring foundation is that it positions the company as a go-to for a wide range of new opportunities, from defense-driven demand to AI data center-led demand, including space-based data centers. While satellites traditionally gather raw data, which can be slow and expensive, the incorporation of Nvidia GPUs into the Owl fleet enables AI to identify, classify, and selectively send the intelligence needed. Additionally, PL's R&D partnership with Google to use its TPUs in space to run AI models on satellites has strengthened PL's differentiated edge.

Essentially, PL is using AI at the edge to achieve on-orbit processing. What we like about PL is its use of an “agile aerospace” approach, meaning it launches fleets of small, low-cost satellites that continuously monitor the globe. PL needs less cost to keep a full-scale 360-degree overview. This, combined with analytics that turn this data into intelligence, makes for very attractive economics for PL’s satellite-as-a-service model and the margin expansion it could bring as contract wins multiply. While current fundamentals don't fully justify the premium valuation, we think PL holds an unmatched monopoly in its goldmine of data, which will push the stock higher in the long term, but timing is everything, and we think, at current levels, too many of the near-term positives are still priced into the stock. Hence, we give the stock a Hold rating.

The stock is trading at a forward EV/Sales of 24.96x versus the group average of 2.41x. The price-to-book ratio is also far higher than the group average at 22x versus 4x. PL trades at a premium multiple, even when compared to others in the peer group, such as BlackSky (BKSY), which trades at a forward EV/Sales of 7.58x. The market is treating PL as an AI-driven software platform with a monopoly on daily earth imaging. PL is far from cheap, particularly as the market is pricing in this AI premium, which has intensified since the company’s acquisition of Bedrock Research last year to accelerate its AI solutions.

Near-term positives are priced in?

It was the 3QFY26 print, in December, that put PL's market opportunity in perspective for investors. In the couple of days following the earnings release, the stock surged almost 40%. For the quarter, PL reported revenue of $81M, up 33% Y/Y and 10% Q/Q, and RPO grew an impressive 361% Y/Y to $672M, while backlog grew 216% Y/Y to $734M. Management also raised FY26 guidance to $297M to $301M and guided Q4 revenue to $76M to $80M, versus estimates currently at $78.17M, with margins in the range of 50% to 52%.

The market was uber bullish on the results, driven specifically by a defense tailwind around PL’s business. Defense & Intelligence revenue grew 70% Y/Y and 15% sequentially due to contract wins (including the Luno B program by the National Geospatial Intelligence Agency, a contract renewal with the National Reconnaissance Office, and a renewed U.S. Navy contract). In comparison, Civil Government revenue was pretty flat, up only 1% Y/Y and 15% Q/Q. Commercial revenue was modestly down Y/Y and sequentially. The chart below from the company’s 10Q shows the Defense & Intelligence segment’s momentum.

PL’s Defense & Intelligence segment has been making up an increasingly larger percentage of total revenue since the start of FY26, as shown below. In FY2025, Defense & Intelligence accounted for 48% of total revenue, and it is well on its way to making up +50% of total revenue in FY26, according to our estimates. The company has secured nine-figure, multi-year contracts with the Swedish Armed Forces; a multimillion-dollar deal with the German government; collaborations with Japan’s Sky Perfect JSAT; an enterprise-scale deal with the Slovenian government; and other contracts with the U.S. Navy, NASA, and others over the past 12 months. All these positives have been comfortably priced into the stock, expectations, and its now hefty valuation. The company has seen four upward EPS revisions over the past three months and nine upward revenue revisions versus only three downward EPS revisions.

When it comes to financials, PL is also not profitable yet on a GAAP basis, with the company reporting a net loss of about ~$59M in 3Q26. That said, the company is on the right track. For the quarter, PL achieved positive adjusted EBITDA of about $5.6M, and FY26 is guiding for positive adjusted EBITDA between $6M and $8M. Q4 marked the fourth consecutive quarter of positive adjusted EBITDA. The company is generating positive free cash flow, meaning it’s building the habit of having more cash coming in than going out, which is notable considering this is a pretty high CapEx business. While the positive adjusted EBITDA and free cash flow are leading signals for profitability to come, this too has been priced into the stock post Q3 print.

PL remains very much a work in progress, and its main growth driver (defense) is vulnerable to changes in global defense spending. Current forecasts expect the global space militarization market to exceed $63B this year and defense communication intelligence to reach $23.34B. There is little doubt that PL is well-positioned to benefit from the expanded TAM in both markets. However, if we see a hiccup in defense spending momentum, it could threaten over 60% of the company’s total revenue, given its premium valuation leaves little to no room for slip-ups.

Bottom Line

At current levels, PL has a huge market opportunity on its hands, and simultaneously, its valuation has stretched beyond the fundamentals. There are two narratives forming around PL. The bull case places the stock price target between $26 and $33 per share and attributes upside to the company’s data monopoly; no one else has the vast amount of daily earth imaging that PL has under its belt. On top of that, there is PL’s attractive operating leverage, which allows it to sell data to multiple customers once the satellite is in orbit without extra cost.

On the other hand, the bear case argues for a price target between $11 and $16 per share. The logic behind the bear case is that it takes money to get those satellites up in space, i.e., more CapEx. This also raises the potential dilution risk, which has been put on the radar after the company raised $460M in convertible debt. Even if we set expenses aside, the stock carries a higher risk profile given the concentration of revenue from defense spending. If the ball falls on defense, Commercial and Civil Government revenue won’t be able to fill the gap without a reset of expectations and, in turn, the stock price. Hence, given that near-term catalysts have been comfortably priced into the stock, we initiate PL with a Hold rating and recommend investors stay on the sidelines for 1H26.

data.title: Over-Rotating In The Rotational Correction
data.description:
Investors bought the dip of the past few days, looking for bargains, despite the release of the minutes from the latest Fed meeting, which suggested some board members see rate hikes as more likely than cuts. I think that is very unlikely, which is why the market looked the other way. Energy and materials led the charge on the tailcoats of higher oil prices, while the technology sector rebounded on news that Meta Platforms plans to buy a gazillion chips from Nvidia over the next several years. The most important reason for the market’s rebound was that we had some better-than-expected economic data.

In December, homebuilders started construction on residential properties at the fastest pace in five months, increasing the annualized rate to 1.4 million. The number of single-family homes rose to the highest number since last February. Increasing supply should help further moderate home prices. One caveat is that milder weather boosted the number. Still, we saw starts in 2025 decline for a fourth consecutive year. While January’s weather probably slowed progress, government policies (and strong encouragement) support what should be the first year that we see starts rise. This is a lever of economic growth that has not been pulled for a long time.

Another positive on the housing front is that wage growth at 3.7% is finally exceeding home price increases, which are now just 1.4% based on the most recent Case-Shiller Home Price Index. This improves affordability.

Lastly, borrowing costs are coming down with the 30-year fixed rate mortgage at 6.09% last week. If we can break below 6%, I think that might trigger more meaningful activity in the existing and new homes sales markets. The bottom line is that wage growth, home prices, and borrowing costs are all moving in a direction that points to an increase in activity this year.

We also had good news on industrial production in January, which increased 0.7% for the month and 2.4% over the past year. That is the largest monthly increase in nearly a year, driven by strength in the manufacturing of business equipment and consumer goods. The new tax advantages for companies to spend on capital equipment may play a role here, as well as post-holiday inventory rebuilding. Regardless, it is consistent with the increase in expansion for the ISM Manufacturing Index and serves as another positive rate of change.

None of these datapoints on their own is that significant, but collectively they show a positive rate of change that supports a continuation of the expansion. Investors have had mixed messages from the high-frequency economic data since the start of this year, as well as valuation concerns in the tech space, which led to more defensive positioning in this rotational correction. The problem I see now is that this rotation has hit an extreme for more defensive sectors like utilities (XLU) and consumer staples (XLP). Meanwhile, bargains are starting to appear in the rubble left over from the AI-induced sell-off. What encourages me is that stocks and sectors are trading in a non-correlated fashion every day. There are lots of winners and lots of losers. This indicates the bull market is healthy for now. When correlations run high, investors are losing their conviction, and it is typically a warning sign of trouble ahead.

data.title: Introducing The Global X Income Edge℠ ETF Suite
data.description:
Implementing options-based strategies has become an increasingly common tool in income-oriented portfolios. Over time, Global X has developed a range of approaches within this space, reflecting different investor objectives, market environments, and preferences for regular distributions versus upside participation.

The Global X Income Edge℠ ETF suite builds on this foundation.

On February 18, 2026, Global X listed the Global X U.S. 500 Income Edge℠ ETF (EDGX) and the Global X Nasdaq-100® Income Edge℠ ETF (EDGQ) on NYSE Arca. These funds maintain exposure to the Solactive GBS United States 500 Index and Nasdaq-100® Index, respectively, and write weekly call options against that exposure in a systematic, actively managed strategy designed to target defined annualized distribution rates – 9% for EDGX and 13% for EDGQ – while retaining the ability to participate in a portion of the respective equity market’s upside.

Broadening Depth of the Options Market Reflects Demand for Sophisticated Strategies

The expansion of the U.S. options market has increased the feasibility of more dynamic, rules-based income strategies within an ETF wrapper. Annual option volumes have increased materially over the past decade, reflecting rising investor demand for instruments to pursue income, hedging, and tactical positioning.

Alongside higher volumes, the composition of the options market has shifted meaningfully toward shorter-dated contracts. Weekly options have become increasingly liquid and widely adopted, providing market participants with more frequent opportunities to adjust exposure.

For investors, these developments increase the feasibility of implementing dynamic options-based income strategies within an ETF structure. Strategies that may otherwise require frequent trading, ongoing monitoring, and operational infrastructure can be delivered through a professionally managed, exchange-traded vehicle.

Systematic Framework for Targeting Defined Distributions

EDGX and EDGQ seek to generate income by maintaining long exposure to their respective reference assets and writing weekly call options in exchange for option premiums. Rather than relying on a fixed or predetermined level of option coverage, the funds employ a systematic process that adjusts the percentage of the portfolio covered each week in pursuit of their distribution rate objectives.

Each week, the funds seek to generate approximately 1/52nd of their stated annual distribution rate target, using option coverage as the primary lever to do so. At the start of each option cycle, the portfolio managers assess prevailing market conditions – including equity volatility, interest rates, and option pricing – to determine the amount of premium required for that week. This premium may be complemented with dividends received from the underlying equity holdings, seeking to achieve the desired weekly distribution.

When volatility is elevated, higher premiums may allow the funds to meet their weekly distribution targets while covering a smaller portion of the portfolio, leaving more exposure uncapped and available to participate in possible equity upside. Conversely, when volatility is lower, option premiums tend to compress, and a greater degree of coverage may be applied to achieve the targeted level of distribution.

By recalibrating coverage on a weekly basis, the Income Edge℠ ETFs seek to internalize what might otherwise be a tactical coverage decision for investors, adjusting exposure as market conditions evolve.

The annualized distribution rate targets of EDGX and EDGQ – 9% and 13%, respectively – reflect the historical volatility profiles of their respective reference assets. The Nasdaq-100 has typically exhibited higher volatility than broader U.S. equity benchmarks, a profile that has historically supported greater option premium potential. As a result, EDGQ is structured with a higher annualized distribution rate target than EDGX, while both funds operate within a consistent investment framework.

This approach reflects the well-documented relationship between option premiums and market volatility. Historically, periods of elevated volatility have been associated with higher option premiums, while calmer markets have generally produced lower levels of income from option writing.

Variable Option Coverage and Weekly Implementation

In a conventional covered call strategy, an investor typically holds a reference asset and writes call options against that position, generating income in exchange for capping a portion of the reference asset’s upside. The degree to which upside is capped depends largely on how much of the portfolio is covered by call options, with higher coverage generally increasing income potential while limiting participation in rising markets.

Beyond the coverage ratio, volatility is a key driver of option premiums, but it does not evolve smoothly over time. As shown below, volatility levels observed on traditional monthly option roll dates have historically been uneven, often fluctuating meaningfully from one expiration to the next.

The level of volatility present on that specific date can have an outsized influence on option premiums and portfolio positioning for the weeks that follow. In environments where volatility changes rapidly, this dynamic can leave premium generation more dependent on the timing of the roll itself.

The Income Edge℠ ETFs are designed to manage this trade-off. Rather than maintaining a fixed level of option coverage, the funds may dynamically adjust coverage levels in response to market conditions. This structure is intended to support income generation while maintaining exposure to equity price movements across varying volatility regimes. We expect the fund’s coverage ratio to average approximately 25% over the long term, though actual coverage may fluctuate meaningfully from week to week and is not fixed, targeted, or guaranteed. In periods of elevated volatility, option coverage may compress well below 25%, while in lower-volatility environments, coverage may expand well above 25%.

A key enabler of this approach is the use of weekly call options. Shorter-dated contracts allow the portfolio managers to reassess market conditions and recalibrate coverage more frequently, reducing reliance on any single option roll date.

The potential relevance of this structure was evident in 2025, when the Nasdaq-100 experienced sharp, event-driven moves. In early April following Liberation Day, the Nasdaq-100 declined 12.71% from April 2 to April 8, 2025, before rebounding 14.53% on a total return basis through the end of the month.1 In such environments, strategies that reset option exposure more frequently may be better able from a portfolio management perspective to respond to rapidly changing conditions, rather than remaining tied to a monthly roll established prior to the event.

By combining weekly option writing with variable coverage, the Income Edge℠ ETFs seek to internalize what might otherwise require ongoing tactical decision-making by the investor, adjusting option coverage as volatility and market conditions evolve within a disciplined, rules-based framework.

Conclusion: Defined Annualized Distribution Rate Targets Within a Systematic Covered Call Strategy

The Global X Income Edge℠ ETFs build on Global X’s lineup of covered call funds by introducing a weekly option strategy designed to target defined annualized distribution rates. By pairing systematic call writing with a flexible coverage approach, EDGX and EDGQ aim to deliver distributions while maintaining exposure to equity market movements. In doing so, the Income Edge℠ ETFs offer investors an additional way to pursue equity income within the Global X covered call framework.

Footnotes

Bloomberg L.P. Retrieved January 28th, 2026.

Glossary

Cboe Nasdaq -100 BuyWrite V2 Index: An index designed to measure the total return of a covered call strategy on the Nasdaq-100 Index. The index reflects a strategy that holds a portfolio replicating the Nasdaq-100 while systematically selling (writing) one-month, at-the-money Nasdaq-100 (NDX) call options. The call options are rolled monthly, on the third Friday of each month.

Cboe Nasdaq 100 Volatility Index (VXN): Commonly referred to as VXN, reflects a market estimate of future volatility of the Nasdaq 100, based on the weighted average of the implied volatilities of the same index.

Correlation: Correlation indicates the strength of the linear relationship between two different variables. It is measured from -1 to 1. A correlation that is greater than zero indicates a positive relationship. A value that is less than zero signifies an inverse relationship. A value closer to 1 or -1 indicates a strong relationship whereas a value of zero indicates no relationship between the two variables.

Distribution Rate: The annual rate an investor would receive if the most recent fund distribution remained the same going forward. The rate represents a single distribution from the fund and does not represent total return of the fund. The distribution rate is calculated by annualizing the most recent distribution and dividing by the most recent fund NAV.

Roll Date: The specific day or period when traders close out positions in an expiring contract and simultaneously open new positions in a contract with a later maturity date.

Solactive GBS United States 500 Index: Market index tracking performance of the largest 500 companies in the U.S. stock market based on market capitalization, weighted by free float market capitalization.

Investing involves risk, including the possible loss of principal. The funds are subject to certain principal risks, including: Active Management Risk; Derivatives Risk; Depositary Receipts Risk; Equity Securities Risk; ETF Investment Risk; Large-Capitalization Companies Risk; Covered Call Option Writing Risk; Cybersecurity Risk; FLEX Options Risk; Risks Related to Investing in the Information Technology Sector; Risk of Investing in Developed Markets and the United States; Issuer Risk; Market Risk; New Fund Risk; Non-Diversification Risk; Operational Risk; Options Premium Tax Risk; Risks Associated with Exchange-Traded Funds, including Authorized Participants Concentration Risk, Large Shareholder Risk, Listing Standards Risk, and Market Trading Risks and Premium/Discount Risks; Trading Halt Risk; Turnover Risk; and Valuation Risk. EDGQ is also subject to Depositary Receipts Risk; Risks Related to Investing in the Semiconductors and Semiconductor Equipment Industry; and Foreign Securities Risk.

There is no assurance the Fund will achieve its target annualized distribution rates. The actual distributions the Fund makes over a one-year period may deviate from its intended target annualized distribution rate. Concentration in a particular industry or sector will subject the funds to loss due to adverse occurrences that may affect that industry or sector. Investors in the funds should be willing to accept a high degree of volatility in the price of the fund’s shares and the possibility of significant losses.

The funds engage in options trading. An option is a contract sold by one party to another that gives the buyer the right, but not the obligation, to buy (call) or sell (put) a stock at an agreed upon price within a certain period or on a specific date. A covered call option involves holding a long position in a particular asset and writing a call option on that same asset with the goal of realizing additional income from the option premium. By selling covered call options, the fund limits its opportunity to profit from an increase in the price of the underlying index above the exercise price, but continues to bear the risk of a decline in the index. A liquid market may not exist for options held by the fund. While the fund receives premiums for writing the call options, the price it realizes from the exercise of an option could be substantially below the index’s current market price.

Shares of ETFs are bought and sold at market price (not NAV) and are not individually redeemed from the Fund. Brokerage commissions will reduce returns.

Carefully consider the funds’ investment objectives, risks, and charges and expenses before investing. This and other information can be found in the funds’ summary or full prospectus. Please read the prospectus carefully before investing.

Global X Management Company LLC serves as an advisor to Global X Funds. The Funds are distributed by SEI Investments Distribution Co. (SIDCO), which is not affiliated with Global X Management Company LLC or Mirae Asset Global Investments.
